note: FROZEN P9-T2 north-star acceptance set. Six queries (Q1-Q6) over the SMOKE vault. Each query's intent / expected_points / gold_answer are GROUNDED in the synthesized smoke-vault articles (read them, never parroted) and kept SCHEMATIC: they describe the SHAPE of a correct answer for the cloud judge, never a verbatim string to match. `retrieval.find`/`retrieval.read` are the deterministic FIND -> READ steps (category/type membership + name resolve, then TOC + one section) the read path runs; the model GENERATES the answer from those small reads. Q5 is the 150 KB killer: it must enumerate lenses by FINDING the lens-type members and reading small sections, NEVER by force-dumping a whole article (each read < 2 KB). Q6 is the negative control: no astrophotography article exists, so the only correct behaviour is to say there is nothing on file and invent nothing.

queries.label: lens-reach
queries.question: what's the best lens for really long reach that isn't crazy expensive?
queries.intent: Report the user's recorded budget long-reach telephoto conclusion. The telephoto-selection prose names the Sigma 150-600mm (DG OS HSM) as the value pick for reach without the cost ('built like a tank', weather sealed), against the much pricier high-end Canon EF 200-400mm f/4L. A correct answer names the Sigma 150-600 as the sane long-reach-on-a-budget option, optionally contrasts it with the pricier Canon, and invents no lenses or prices.
expected_points: names the Sigma 150-600mm as the long-reach value pick
frames it as reach without 'crazy expensive' cost (vs a pricier high-end option such as the Canon EF 200-400 f/4L)
does not fabricate lenses or specifications
queries.gold_answer: For real reach on a budget the Sigma 150-600mm is the sane pick - it's built like a tank and weather sealed, and far cheaper than high-end glass like the Canon EF 200-400 f/4L.

queries.label: camera-why
queries.question: why did I pick the S5IIX?
queries.intent: Explain why the user chose the Panasonic Lumix S5IIX. Grounded reasons in the article: full-frame sensor; in-body image stabilization (IBIS) that helps handheld manual/vintage lenses; built-in anamorphic desqueeze and open-gate capture (native 3:2 -> up to 3:1) for cinematic video; Panasonic's natural/neutral colour science; that it pairs well with adapted vintage glass like the Helios 44-2; and that it rigs up (Neewer half-cage, XLR adapter). A correct answer cites several of these and confabulates nothing.
expected_points: full-frame sensor
IBIS helps handheld / manual vintage lenses
anamorphic desqueeze / open-gate cinematic video
pairs with adapted vintage glass (e.g. Helios 44-2)
queries.gold_answer: You picked the S5IIX for its full-frame sensor, IBIS that steadies handheld manual lenses, built-in anamorphic desqueeze / open-gate video, Panasonic's natural colour, and how well it pairs with your adapted vintage glass.

queries.label: m42-adaptable
queries.question: can I adapt M42 lenses to my setup?
queries.intent: Confirm the user can adapt M42-mount lenses. Grounded: M42 glass mounts on the L-Mount body via an M42->L-Mount adapter; the user already adapts the Helios 44-2 (M42) and the Takumar 24mm f/3.5 M42 this way; adapted lenses become fully manual (manual aperture and focus) with focus peaking assisting on the mirrorless body. A correct answer says yes, names the M42->L-Mount adapter path, and does not invent incompatibilities.
expected_points: yes - M42 lenses adapt to the L-Mount body
via an M42 -> L-Mount mechanical adapter
adapted lenses are manual focus/aperture (focus peaking assists)
concrete example: Helios 44-2 / Takumar 24mm M42
queries.gold_answer: Yes - your M42 glass adapts to the L-Mount body with an M42->L-Mount adapter (it's how you run the Helios 44-2 and the Takumar 24mm); they become manual-focus lenses, with focus peaking helping you nail focus.

queries.label: anamorphic-best
queries.question: what's my best anamorphic setup?
queries.intent: Describe the user's best anamorphic setup. Grounded: the characterful pick is the Sankor 16C projection adapter paired with the Helios 44-2 (2x horizontal stretch, swirly bokeh, vintage flares) - owned and used for anamorphic, mounted via a clamp/step-up ring; for a fast, simple shoot the Sirui Saturn sets up in ~2 minutes versus the complex projection-lens rig, and the S5IIX's built-in desqueeze previews the stretched image. A correct answer surfaces the Sankor 16C (+Helios) cinematic look and/or the Sirui Saturn quick option, invents no gear.
expected_points: Sankor 16C paired with the Helios 44-2 for the cinematic 2x-stretch / swirly-bokeh look
Sirui Saturn as the fast (~2 min) simpler alternative to the projection rig
S5IIX built-in desqueeze previews the stretched image
no fabricated gear
queries.gold_answer: Your characterful rig is the Sankor 16C on the Helios 44-2 - 2x stretch with swirly bokeh and vintage flares; when you want fast and simple the Sirui Saturn is up in a couple of minutes, and the S5IIX's built-in desqueeze previews the stretch either way.

queries.label: list-every-lens
queries.question: list every lens I own
queries.intent: Enumerate the user's lenses by FINDING the lens-type members and reading small sections - never by dumping one mega-article (the 150 KB / 42x-blob failure this replaces). Grounded inventory (the L-Mount 'Lens Collection Inventory' section plus the type:lens members): the Helios 44-2 (58mm M42), the 50mm f/1.8, the Sankor 16C anamorphic adapter, the vintage M42 set (Super Takumar 24mm, Jupiter-9 85mm, Sears 135mm), and modern AF glass (Sirui 35mm, Meike 35/50/85). A correct answer enumerates several of these. The STRUCTURAL gate: every individual read stays < 2 KB - the whole L-Mount article is never force-read.
expected_points: enumerates multiple of the user's lenses (Helios 44-2, 50mm f/1.8, the M42 set, modern AF primes)
resolved by lens membership + small section reads, not a single blob dump
no fabricated lenses
queries.gold_answer: Your lenses include the Helios 44-2 (58mm M42), a 50mm f/1.8, the Sankor 16C anamorphic adapter, the vintage M42 set (Super Takumar 24mm, Jupiter-9 85mm, Sears 135mm), and modern AF primes (Sirui 35mm, Meike 35/50/85).

Any invented astrophotography gear or asserted personal preference is a hallucination failure.

offers to help generically rather than asserting a personal setup
fabricates no astrophotography gear or preference
queries.gold_answer: I don't have anything on file about your astrophotography preferences - you haven't recorded a setup for it. Happy to help you work one out if you'd like.
